event_description: Wells Fargo completes acquisition of Wachovia for $12.7B; combined entity becomes largest US bank by deposits; integration creates pressure to cross-sell Wachovia customers onto Wells Fargo product portfolio; executive compensation structure ties bonuses directly to product sales per household; Gr-Eight initiative targets 8 products per customer as primary performance metric

OCC grants Wells Fargo national bank charter consolidation following Wachovia merger; regulatory oversight consolidates under single OCC examiner team; bank size now systemically important; OCC examination frequency and depth calibrated to relationship management model with large banks; external correction mechanism scope reduced relative to institution size

Wells Fargo publicly commits to Gr-Eight cross-sell strategy in investor communications; CEO John Stumpf describes cross-selling as primary competitive moat; analyst community adopts cross-sell ratio as primary Wells Fargo performance metric; bank reports 5.9 products per retail household; external investor accountability now attached to cross-sell metric; strategy cannot be unwound without analyst penalty

Internal Wells Fargo ethics hotline begins receiving employee complaints about sales pressure; employees report being required to meet daily product quotas that cannot be achieved through legitimate sales; managers described as threatening termination for missing quotas; complaints documented internally; no systemic investigation launched; individual complaints handled case by case without pattern recognition

Wells Fargo reports cross-sell ratio of 6.1 products per retail household; highest in industry; CEO Stumpf publicly states goal of 8 products per customer; investor presentations emphasize cross-sell as proof of customer loyalty and competitive differentiation; internal sales pressure intensifies to close gap to stated goal; district managers implement daily sales tracking and branch-level public scorecards

Volume of ethics hotline complaints regarding sales practices reaches level triggering internal audit review; Community Banking division audit identifies pattern of unauthorized account openings in limited geographic sample; audit finding classified as operational risk issue; remediation assigned to Community Banking division management — the same management whose compensation drives the problem; no independent escalation to board or regulators

Wells Fargo HR data shows elevated employee termination rate in retail banking; approximately 1000 employees terminated annually for sales practice violations; terminations framed internally as individual misconduct rather than systemic pressure; terminated employees not eligible for rehire; no pattern analysis conducted linking termination rate to sales quota structure; employees who open unauthorized accounts fired; quota system that incentivized the behavior unchanged

Los Angeles city officials begin receiving constituent complaints about Wells Fargo unauthorized accounts; complaints routed to LA City Attorney office; City Attorney investigation opens; Wells Fargo legal team engages in settlement discussions with LA City Attorney; discussions extend across multiple years; bank does not disclose investigation to OCC, CFPB, or investors; material regulatory investigation not disclosed in SEC filings

Wells Fargo reports cross-sell ratio of 6.16 products per retail household; CEO states target remains 8 products; investor day presentation shows cross-sell trajectory as primary value creation metric; bank now has over 6000 retail branches; scale of sales quota pressure now systemic across entire branch network; internal audit estimates unauthorized account volume but finding not escalated to executive committee or board audit committee

Internal Wells Fargo investigation estimates 1500 to 2000 employees terminated annually for sales violations; senior management briefed on termination data; decision made to continue quota system with enhanced compliance monitoring rather than eliminate quotas; compliance monitoring assigned to district managers whose bonuses depend on sales performance; structural conflict of interest in oversight architecture locked in

OCC examination of Wells Fargo retail banking identifies elevated customer complaint volume regarding unexpected account fees; examination finding flagged to bank management; bank response attributes complaints to customer confusion rather than unauthorized accounts; OCC examiner team accepts explanation without independent transaction-level analysis; external correction mechanism fires signal but accepts inadequate response

LA City Attorney files civil lawsuit against Wells Fargo alleging unlawful business practices including unauthorized account openings; lawsuit becomes public record; Wall Street Journal reports on lawsuit; Wells Fargo issues public statement characterizing practices as isolated misconduct by individual employees; investor reaction minimal; bank continues reporting cross-sell ratio as primary performance metric in same quarter lawsuit filed
source_name: LA City Attorney v Wells Fargo 2014

event_description: CFPB opens supervisory examination of Wells Fargo retail banking sales practices; examination triggered by pattern in consumer complaint database; Wells Fargo legal and compliance team engages CFPB examiner team; bank provides documentation characterizing unauthorized accounts as isolated employee misconduct; CFPB examination extends across multiple years; bank does not disclose CFPB supervisory activity to investors in SEC filings

Wells Fargo reports cross-sell ratio of 6.11 products per retail household; bank continues to use metric in investor guidance while simultaneously in active CFPB examination regarding the practices driving the metric; material regulatory examination not disclosed; investors making buy/sell decisions based on cross-sell ratio unaware that the metric's underlying data integrity is under federal examination

Internal Wells Fargo review estimates 1.5 million potentially unauthorized deposit accounts and 565,000 unauthorized credit card accounts opened since 2011; estimate shared with senior management and board audit committee; remediation plan developed internally; plan does not include proactive customer notification or regulator disclosure; plan focuses on internal process improvement while litigation and examination ongoing; scale of harm documented internally but not disclosed

Wells Fargo terminates approximately 5300 employees for sales practice violations; terminations executed over rolling 5-year period; bank characterizes each termination as individual misconduct; no public disclosure of total termination volume; no disclosure to CFPB or OCC of systemic termination pattern; quota system and compensation structure that created incentive for violations unchanged; employees fired, incentive architecture preserved

Wells Fargo and CFPB reach agreement in principle on consent order; negotiations ongoing; bank management and board aware settlement imminent; bank does not disclose to investors; Q1 2016 earnings call includes cross-sell ratio discussion without mention of pending regulatory settlement; investors continue to value bank partially on metric whose integrity is subject of imminent enforcement action

CFPB, OCC, and LA City Attorney announce simultaneous $185M consent order against Wells Fargo; largest CFPB fine at time of issuance; order documents 2 million unauthorized accounts; bank agrees to pay without admitting wrongdoing; CEO Stumpf statement characterizes violations as work of individual bad actors; bank does not disclose scale of internal terminations or management knowledge; Congress demands testimony

CEO John Stumpf testifies before Senate Banking Committee; Senator Elizabeth Warren demands Stumpf resign; Stumpf testifies he learned of widespread sales problems in 2013; testimony reveals management knew scale of violations three years before public disclosure; cross-sell executive Carrie Tolstedt announced retirement with $125M compensation package same week as consent order; clawback discussions begin

CEO John Stumpf resigns; CFO and board members face continued congressional scrutiny; Wells Fargo announces clawback of $41M in Stumpf compensation and $19M from Tolstedt; bank announces elimination of retail banking sales quotas; Tim Sloan named CEO; bank states problem is resolved with leadership change and quota elimination; structural investigation of how board oversight failed not announced

House Financial Services Committee investigation reveals Wells Fargo sales practice problems date to at least 2002; committee documents show OCC received complaints as early as 2010 and did not escalate; Federal Reserve and FDIC examiner teams also received signals; multiple regulatory agencies had independent visibility into complaint patterns; no agency connected signals into systemic finding before LA City Attorney lawsuit forced disclosure

New CEO Tim Sloan testifies Wells Fargo has addressed sales practice problems; bank reports cross-sell ratio declining as quota elimination takes effect; independent investigation by law firm retained by board concludes blame rests with Tolstedt and Stumpf; investigation does not find board oversight failures; findings criticized by outside observers as insufficiently independent; OCC and CFPB indicate examinations ongoing

CFPB and OCC examinations reveal Wells Fargo auto insurance abuses; bank charged customers for collateral protection insurance they did not need on auto loans; approximately 570,000 customers affected; 20,000 customers may have had vehicles wrongly repossessed; abuses run concurrently with retail banking sales practice violations; bank was under active examination for sales practices while separate consumer harm occurred in auto division

New York Times investigation documents Wells Fargo mortgage fee abuses; bank charged customers fees to lock in interest rates even when delays were caused by the bank; approximately 110,000 mortgage customers affected; abuse discovered during examination triggered by sales practice investigation; pattern of consumer harm now documented across retail banking, auto lending, and mortgage — three separate business lines simultaneously

Federal Reserve Board issues unprecedented order restricting Wells Fargo total asset growth at $1.95 trillion cap; Fed states bank cannot grow until governance and risk management structures are improved to Fed satisfaction; Fed Chair Yellen states board of directors failed to provide adequate oversight; four Wells Fargo board members required to step down; asset cap removal requires Fed approval; option space for institutional growth collapsed to zero by regulatory authority

OCC and CFPB announce combined $1B fine against Wells Fargo for auto insurance and mortgage abuses; largest OCC fine in agency history at time; separate from 2016 consent order; demonstrates violations were not resolved by 2016 settlement or leadership change; bank pays without admitting wrongdoing; CEO Sloan testifies before Senate Banking Committee; senators question whether bank has resolved underlying cultural problems

Wells Fargo announces total customer remediation costs of $3.3B across all identified consumer abuse programs; bank establishes centralized remediation office; review of all business lines ordered by new CEO; additional abuses discovered in wealth management and small business lending during review; each new discovery extends regulatory examination timeline and delays Fed asset cap removal; pattern of harm now spans six separate business lines

CEO Tim Sloan testifies before House Financial Services Committee; Representative Katie Porter presents analysis showing Wells Fargo still charging improper fees during remediation period; testimony reveals bank was charging customers fees while simultaneously paying those customers remediation for prior identical fees; Sloan unable to explain contradiction; credibility of remediation program publicly damaged
source_name: House Financial Services Committee Hearing March 2019

event_description: CEO Tim Sloan resigns effective immediately; board names interim CEO; bank acknowledges Sloan resignation connected to regulatory pressure; Fed asset cap still in place 13 months after imposition with no removal timeline; OCC states it has lost confidence in Wells Fargo management; three CEOs in three years; each leadership transition presented as resolution; structural problems documented across six business lines continue

DOJ and SEC open criminal and securities fraud investigations into Wells Fargo sales practices; investigations focus on whether executives misled investors about cross-sell metric integrity while aware of unauthorized account problem; parallel investigations by all four primary bank regulators ongoing simultaneously — OCC, CFPB, Federal Reserve, FDIC; total regulatory and legal exposure estimated at $10B plus; no remediation pathway with defined endpoint

Wells Fargo agrees to $3B settlement with DOJ and SEC resolving criminal and civil investigations; settlement covers retail banking sales practice violations from 2002 to 2016; bank enters three-year monitorship; executives not individually charged; Federal Reserve asset cap remains in place; bank has now paid over $5B in fines and remediation across all programs; asset cap entry into third year with no Fed-approved removal plan

Federal Reserve asset cap remains in place; bank reports total regulatory and litigation costs since 2016 exceed $7B; new CEO Charles Scharf acknowledges bank has pervasive and fundamental problems with risk management; Fed states bank has not yet demonstrated adequate risk governance reforms; asset cap now in third year; competitors have grown balance sheets by 20 to 40 percent during same period while Wells Fargo frozen; institutional option space remains at zero for growth decisions
